data.title: AMD: Trillion-Dollar Market Cap In Sight - Upgrade To Buy
data.description:
I previously covered Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) in October 2025, discussing how the company had given away 10% of its equity to drive OpenAI's future chip purchases, with the unconventional/dilutive growth strategy designed to balance the intensifying competition from Nvidia (NVDA)/Broadcom (AVGO).

Despite the numerous tailwinds arising from the OpenAI deal across AMD's future top-line performance and ROCm platform ecosystem gains, I had believed that the stock had been overly buoyed then, with the expensive valuations and the pulled forward upside potential triggering my Hold rating then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am upgrading the AMD stock to a Buy, thanks to its robust growth tailwinds across the expanding x86 market share, the improving data center monetization opportunities, and the ambitious long-term targets - with these signaling the potential expansion in its market capitalization to over $1T over the next few years.

AMD Jump Starts Its x86/Data Center Monetization

AMD 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Hold rating, AMD has mostly traded sideways between the established Q3 '25 support levels of $210s and the resistance levels of $265s, with it signaling the notably priced in AI exuberance.

This is especially since the stock has barely moved despite the double beat FQ3 '25 performance, the promising FQ4 '25 guidance, and the ambitious long-term target offered in the Analyst Day 2025.

On the other hand, at a time when the AI bubble has seemingly burst and some of its semiconductor peers have notably pulled back by the double-digits, I am already impressed by AMD's growing bullish support at current levels, with the recent sideways trading cadence presenting a great insight for those looking to add.

1. Growing x86 Market Share

AMD has reported overall x86 shipment market share gains to 30.9% in Q3 '25 (+5.9 points YoY), as they also drive improved revenue results while

"setting new highs in both desktop and server segments thanks to rising sales of premium processors and the ramp of its newest platforms."

Part of the tailwinds may be attributed the new PC refresh cycle across the enterprise/commercial/gaming end markets arising from "the approaching end-of-support for Microsoft Windows 10 in October 2025," as Counterpoint reports growing global PC shipments by +8.1% YoY in Q3 '25.

Given that approximately 40% of the current x86 installed base are still on Windows 10, I am of the opinion that AMD is likely to report robust PC-related revenues moving forward.

The same has been observed in its notably higher Client revenues of $2.75B in FQ3 '25 (+46.2% YoY) and Gaming revenues of $1.29B (+180.4% YoY), along with the drastically richer Client and Gaming Segment operating margins of 21.4% (+9.2 points YoY).

This development may also be partly attributed to Intel's (INTC) ongoing "capacity constraints, especially on Intel 10 and Intel 7 limited our ability to fully meet demand in Q3 for both data center and client products," which naturally contributed to AMD's outsized market share gains and higher PC-related revenues in the Q3 '25 earnings season.

2. Data Center Opportunities Grow

Based on the YTD revenue growth by +34.4% YoY and the richer FQ3'25 adj gross margin performance at 54% (+11 points/inline YoY), it is unsurprising that AMD has also felt confident to offer the robust FQ4'25 revenue guidance of $9.6B (+25.4% YoY) and adj gross margin guidance of 54.5% (+0.5 points YoY).

Most importantly, readers must note that these numbers have yet to include "any revenue from AMD Instinct MI308 shipments to China," with further upsides rather likely, based on the recent "export approval for its Instinct MI308 AI chips."

Given that NVDA's GPUs are still banned in China, AMD may possibly report an outsized FQ4'25 data center performance beyond FQ3'25 levels of $4.34B (+33.9% QoQ/+22.5% YoY).

For FY2026, AMD is likely to benefit from the hyperscalers' large scale data center MI300/MI350/MI400 Series deployments as well, aided by the vertically integrated and rapidly scalable Helios rack-scale AI solutions across silicon, software, systems, power, and cooling capabilities.

This is on top of the strategic partnerships with OpenAI (OPENAI) and Oracle (ORCL), as AMD hints at over $100B in cumulative revenues over the next few years, aided by the growing custom design wins of $45B.

Readers must not forget AMD's expanding server x86 CPU market share of 27.8% in Q3 '25 (+3.6 points YoY), thanks to the robust hyperscaler demand for its high performance Turin CPU and 2026 likely to bring forth a similar tailwind:

A number of our large hyperscale clients are now forecasting significant CPU build into 2026... it's a positive demand environment, and it is because AI is requiring quite a bit of general-purpose compute...

It catches our cycle as we're ramping Turin. So the Turin ramp has gone extremely fast, and we see good pull for that product as well as consistent strong demand for our Genoa product line as well...

I do feel like it's durable. It is not a short-term thing. I think it is a multi-quarter phenomenon as we're seeing just much more demand as these AI workloads really turn into -- you have to do real work. (Seeking Alpha.)

3. Ambitious Long-Term Target Triggers Compelling Valuations

AMD's Long-Term Target

These reasons may also be why AMD has offered a relatively ambitious long-term financial target with revenue growth at a CAGR of ~35% to over ~$100B and adj EPS growth at a 4Y CAGR of +42.8% (based on the annualized FQ3'25 adj EPS performance of $4.80 and the management's long-term adj EPS guidance of ~$20).

Given the relatively compelling growth prospects against its 5Y historical profile at +30.8%/+38.9%, respectively, I can understand why the consensus have already raised their forward estimates as the stock also remains well supported despite the recent market-wide meltdown.

AMD Valuations

Given that most of the new orders/backlogs will only be monetized from 2026/2027 onwards, AMD may appear to be somewhat expensive at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 62.26x, up from the 1Y mean of 30.54x, the 5Y mean of 38.57x, and the sector median of 23.38x.

Otherwise, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 62.26x and the management's adj EPS growth estimates at a 4Y CAGR of +42.8%, AMD appears rather compelling at my estimated 4Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.45x, compared to its direct competitors, including:

  • general AI - NVDA at my estimated 4Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.34x (using the same methodology),
  • custom AI - AVGO at 1.48x,
  • custom AI - Marvell (MRVL) at 0.85x,
  • x86 compute/CPU - INTC at 0.12x,
  • ARM compute - Qualcomm (QCOM) at 1.86x,
  • ARM compute - Arm (ARM) at 4.19x.

This is based on the assumption that the Lisa Su-led company is able to deliver the ambitious growth targets as outlined above and thus deserving the premium P/E valuations.

Given that the stock is priced for perfect execution over the next few years, it goes without saying that there is minimal room for error - with it presenting certain risks to its high growth, profitable investment thesis.

AMD Is Upgraded To A Buy

AMD 5Y Stock Price

For now, the bulls continues to support AMD's recent gains thanks to the double beat FQ3 '25 performance, the promising FQ4 '25 guidance, and the ambitious long-term target offered in the Analyst Day 2025, despite the market wide meltdown.

AMD's Technical Indicators

AMD's technical indicators across the reasonable RSIs and the moderating trading volumes also imply its ability to keep much of its recent gains, with the stock likely to remain well supported at its new trading floor of $210s moving forward.

While speculative, based on the management's long-term adj EPS guidance of $20, investors may look forward to an outsized upside potential to my bull-case long-term price target of $771.

Based on the FQ3 '25 share count of 1.64B and the implied market capitalization of $1.26T (up from the current levels of $388.2B), it appears that AMD may eventually join the Trillion-Dollar club over the next few years.

This conjecture is not overly aggressive as well, based on NVDA's current market capitalization of $4.53T and AVGO's at $1.67T, assuming that AMD catches up to its peers and achieves the management's "clear path to double-digit share in the data center AI market."

Does this mean that I am upgrading the AMD stock to a Buy?

Yes indeed, thanks to the robust insights offered by its growing backlog and data center deals with numerous partners.

While AMD is likely to remain a far second in the ongoing AI arms race, I concur with Lisa Su in which the 2030 AI market size of $1T remains big enough for numerous key players, with it presenting the former's compelling investment thesis for those still looking to add AI-related stocks.

data.title: Resist AI FOMO And Stick With Your Long-Term Plan
data.description:
Written by Austin Rogers for High Yield Investor

There's nothing that will derail you from your preconceived investment strategy like seeing others get rich quick.

When other investors are enjoying rapid gains and seemingly effortless success by throwing money at the latest stock market trends, and when this happens for an extended period of time, it is only natural for the thoughtful, fundamentals-based investor to second-guess themselves.

"Am I doing something wrong?"

"Am I a terrible investor? Should I just give up stock-picking and buy the S&P 500 or Nasdaq ETF?"

"Is technology the only way to do well in the stock market today?"

Right now, the US economy (and much of the global economy) is in the grips of an AI gold rush.

It is hard to engage with any form of media, especially financial media, without hearing multiple references to AI. It is now everywhere in our culture. AI-generated content is all over the Internet.

And in the stock market, it is hard not to notice that AI-related stocks (CHAT) are absolutely trouncing everything else, especially income-oriented investments like dividend stocks (SCHD) and REITs (VNQ):

It's easy to look at this chart and conclude that one would be ~67% richer today if they had sold everything else and gone all-in on CHAT at the beginning of this year.

But this way of thinking is entirely based on hindsight. It also assumes that one buys into the hot trend at the right time and (somehow) knows exactly when to sell.

The human mind tends to heavily weigh the importance of recent events and underweight everything else in history. This is called "recency bias."

Recency bias makes an investor think that AI stocks will continue going up because they have done very well this year, and everyone is excited and optimistic about them.

But if we were to assign equal weight to both recent events and the grand scheme of history, we'd have to acknowledge that revolutionary new technologies virtually always cause investors to overvalue and overinvest in them.

This has been the case for railroads (1870s), electricity (1920s), the Internet (late 1990s), crypto/Bitcoin (BTC-USD) (late 2010s), arguably green energy (early 2020s), and now artificial intelligence.

It may be impossible to say with certainty when the assets associated with the new technology become overvalued and overinvested in, and it is equally impossible to say how high the prices of the associated stocks will climb. But historically speaking, they usually (perhaps always) become overvalued eventually.

At some point, it becomes apparent that despite how revolutionary the new technology clearly is, it will take longer than initially expected to widely implement it. At that point, investors begin to realize that they've overinvested in the space and overbuilt.

They've built more miles of railroad tracks than can be profitably utilized. They've produced more electrical infrastructure than can be profitably installed. They've laid more fiber optic cable in the ground than Internet usage requires.

It certainly appears as though we are on track to repeat history with the massive buildout of data centers on which to run AI software.

Moreover, there are some signs that AI has become overinvested and overvalued, similar to what has happened in prior bubbles.

In the 1920s, for example, utility stocks (XLU) soared as electricity spread from 30% of US households at the beginning of the decade to 70% at its close. This euphoria for electricity facilitated a complex and interconnected pyramid scheme of holding companies and subsidiaries. More companies were created based on the same assets, driving more capital into the utility sector and inflating valuations of the actual assets.

In the late 1990s, there was a circular flow of spending within the Internet sub-sector, wherein hardware companies like Cisco (CSCO) provided seller-financing for telecom companies to buy Internet infrastructure equipment from them.

We see something similar happening today.

Like the utility sector in the 1920s, a complex and interconnected ownership structure is forming between the AI-related tech companies. And like the Internet sub-sector in the late 1990s, there is a circular flow of spending within the space that currently accounts for the vast majority of AI-related revenue.

All of this interconnected ownership, circular capex, and rapid buildout of AI infrastructure is premised on the notion that demand for AI products is going to surge exponentially in the coming years.

But what if demand for AI grows a lot more slowly than expected, as it did in the late 1990s and early 2000s?

If that is the case, then the current AI infrastructure capex boom is going to result in a multi-year period of oversupply, just as telecom capex did in the 2000s. Also, peak valuations of the companies involved in AI will prove far too optimistic and will eventually deflate.

This doesn't mean that AI isn't a revolutionary technology. It is.

But like so many other revolutionary new technologies that came before it, from rail transportation to electricity to the Internet, widespread implementation will likely take longer than the early adopters expect it to.

A decade ago, we remember hearing panicked hand-wringing from technologists about how self-driving vehicles would soon put millions of truck and taxi drivers out of work. But here we are, ten years later, with automated vehicles just beginning to enter commercial use and experts predicting that fully automated trucking is still 10-20 years away.

We think a similar degree of hastiness accompanies the thinking behind today's AI infrastructure boom and AI-related company valuations.

We suspect it will take a long time to practically and profitably implement AI. Likewise, we suspect it will take a long time for demand from outside the AI-tech space to grow to the point where it fully utilizes the capacity currently being built.

Stay The Course

Fellow landlords, we encourage you to stay the course. If you have carefully and thoughtfully created an investment plan, stick with it.

Resist recency bias.

Say no to FOMO.

Trust in your long-term strategy.

We are not saying that you ought to have no exposure to the technology sector or to AI-related stocks. If, for example, your preconceived investment plan involves a 20% allocation to tech, then it would be reasonable to stick to that allocation. If you've owned some of the names involved in the AI frenzy for a long time and consider them core holdings, we don't suggest selling.

We are simply writing to remind you of the wisdom and prudence of disciplined, unemotional investing.

Yes, quick riches have been gained in the AI space this year, but riches have also been lost by giving in to FOMO in past asset bubbles.

Enduring wealth is not built by chasing momentum or buying "what's working" at any given time. Rather, it is built by adhering to a consistent, thoughtfully designed, long-term investment strategy that you can stick to through the inevitable ups and downs of the market.

Remember the old parable of the tortoise and the hare.

Investing is a marathon, not a sprint.

While it can be painful in the short term, we believe investors who stay the course and stick with their long-term investment plans will do well in the long run.

data.title: Gencor: Maintaining 'Buy' Rating As We Approach Q4 Earnings
data.description:
Intro

We last wrote on Gencor Industries, Inc. (GENC) in late August this year, when we upgraded the highway construction manufacturer to a 'Buy' shortly after the release of the company's Q3 results (Reported 8/8/2025). Unfortunately, our bullish upgrade coincided with the stock topping out just north of $16 a share. Despite Gencor stock printing an intermediate golden cross earlier in August this year, shares were not able to push on from the point and have been making lower lows ever since. Shares as we stand come in at $13.14, meaning they have declined roughly 19% since our bullish call just under 3 months ago.

As a reminder, Gencor reported an impressive set of Q3 numbers back in August, as the CEO laid out below. Suffice it to say, we believe the long-term bullish fundamentals of Gencor remain intact, which sees shares now trading at a strong discount to its 5-year average comparables and the sector in general.

“Our third quarter brought improved results over the prior year, both in terms of revenue and profits. The overall performance of our first nine months was also solid, with approximately 5% increase in revenue and 14% more operating income over the prior year. As we work toward our fiscal year-end, we expect to continue to track with our stable growth strategy and focus on top-line growth and operational efficiency.”

Specifically, top-line revenues grew by 5.5% which enabled gross profit of $7.1% increase by over 16%. Net income attributable to shareholders came in at $3.8 million for the quarter, which was an excellent 46% improvement over the same period of 12 months previous. What these trends demonstrate is that if Gencor can put a sizable number of orders together in a given period, economies of scale should ensure growing margins right down to GAAP profit over time.

Excellent Valuation

Irrespective of short-term movements in the stock, what Gencor has going for it is its cheap valuation, strong profitability and pristine balance sheet, which continues to carry no debt. Furthermore, with shareholder equity continuing to rise ($209.9 million at the end of Q3), we fail to see how value will not continue being created for qualifying shareholders over time. Suffice it to say, as long as GENC can generate enough sales and earnings, which can consequently build the company's assets, then those very assets can continue to grow returns over time

Valuation GENC Sector Median
Trailing P/E (GAAP) 12.72 23.52
Trailing P/B 0.92 2.91
Trailing P/S 1.64 1.68

Moreover, let's zoom into Gencor's book-multiple because there are many moving parts to a company's balance sheet. Firstly, a P/B of 0.92 means that for every $1 invested in GENC, the investor receives $1.09 of the company's assets. Now, many companies that are trading under book value have low valuations for good reasons: usually because the company's profitability is constrained in some way. Not with Gencor. In fact, the company's stated Return on assets over the past 12 months comes in at 6.9% which again beats the sector median (5.14%) by a healthy margin.

Assets Remain Dirt Cheap

Furthermore, it gets better for value investors here because the company's cash & ST position ($136 million at the end of Q3) makes up more than half of Gencor's assets ($220.2 million). In fact, there are literally zero intangible assets on Gencor's balance sheet, which means the following.

  1. Assets are the roots that birth the tree of every organisation. The more you have of them (and the cheaper you can buy them), the more potential long-term, because they are essentially what causes earnings and sales to take place.
  2. Gencor's real return on assets is much higher than the stated number due to the balance sheet being frontloaded with cash & ST investments (Which contributes nothing to the company's EBIT)
  3. Given the company's zero debt position and absence of goodwill and intangible assets, possibilities for future impairment charges remain very small, protecting the investor's investment over the long term.

Industry-Leading Products

From a tariff standpoint, Gencor remains unaffected because its products (asphalt pavers, hot mix asphalt plants, fluid heat transfer systems, etc.) are manufactured in the US. On the asphalt plant side, Gencor's expertise dates back to 1894, when its subsidiary, Bituma Corporation, successfully transitioned asphalt production to the continuous model. With respect to Asphalt Pavers production, Gencor's Blaw-Know brand is also well over a century old, with this brand's pavers continuing to be the industry-leading choice in this segment. What the above tells us is that even if highway construction levels were to soften somewhat over the next few years, Gencor has the quality to be able to take market share from competitors over time.

Elevated Infrastructure Budgets In the US & Canada

Furthermore, there is no sign as of yet that infrastructure spending is projected to slow down. In the US, for example, the $1.2 trillion infrastructure bill of 2021 provided a very generous $110 billion for the country's roads, highways and bridges.

Furthermore, concerning Canada, we see a similar trend with respect to multiple provinces. Ontario, Alberta, and Quebec have allocated multi-billion-dollar budgets for highway expansion and improvement in the forthcoming years. We acknowledge that the speed at which contractors deliver this work depends largely on how quickly respective governments release funds, but as mentioned, Gencor's proven products as well as its pristine balance sheet are there to protect the company if indeed a contraction were to materialise.

Upcoming Q4 Earnings

As shown below, Gencor's revenues and profits have grown not only in the second quarter of this year but also over the first nine months to date. Although top-line growth has slowed (3.9% trailing, compared to a five-year average of 8.19%), investors should focus on the following as we approach the release of the company's Q4 earnings (Report set to be released in early December).

Value investors many times place too much importance on growth rates, especially when one is dealing with a company such as GENC, which, as mentioned, carries no debt. We state 'over importance' because, given this company's present valuation and strong balance sheet, value will still be created for shareholders even if growth rates remain below historic norms.

Furthermore, investors must take a long-term view here due to the company's micro-cap nature (below-average order count) and seasonal factors. Historically, Gencor's order flow increases between October and February, with shipping volume subsequently remaining elevated until June. Suffice it to say, now that the backlog is more on an even keel ($26.2 million at the end of Q3), we will pay attention to this number in the upcoming report, along with what fiscal 2025 will end up being.

Although almost $24 million was used in operational cash-flow in Q2 (which decreased Gencor's cash-balance temporarily), one mustn't forget that the company's cash & ST position makes up over 75% of the market-cap ($179 million) of the company. Furthermore, over the past 12 months, Gencor's operational cash flow has actually come in positive as lumpy order-flow gets ironed out over longer periods. Therefore, with a fortress cash & ST position, as long as order-flow remains healthy, we believe Gencor over 12-month periods will continue to report cash-flow positive numbers, all things remaining equal.

Conclusion

To sum up, we are reiterating our 'Buy' signal in Gencor Industries as we approach the release of the company's Q4 earnings. Despite the recent downtrend in the stock price, we believe bullish fundamentals, a keen valuation and a pristine balance sheet limit downside risk here considerably. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: JPST: Simple Cash Alternative ETF, Solid Performance And Yield
data.description:
It's been a while since I last covered the JPMorgan Ultra-Short Income ETF (JPST), a simple, effective income ETF. JPST focuses on quality short-term securities, resulting in a 4.5% dividend yield, slightly higher than T-bills, with a bit more in risk and volatility. Overall I rate the fund a buy, although the Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA) and the Alpha Architect 1-3 Month Box ETF (BOXX) both seem slightly superior to JPST.

JPST - Overview and Analysis

Strategy and Portfolio

JPST is an actively-managed fund focusing on short-term, investment-grade corporate bonds and notes, with investments in a couple other types of securities. Asset allocations are as follows:

Credit and Rate Risk

JPST focuses on short-term securities, with an average maturity of 1.0 year, duration of 0.7 years. Interest rate risk is quite close to zero, so expect little in price movement when rates increase or decrease. As an example, JPST's price declined by 0.7% during 2022, compared to double-digit declines for most bonds.

As an aside, duration close to zero is functionally equivalent to zero, because the security or investment simply gets paid back quickly, so losses don't last for a meaningful amount of time. JPST does seem to have a non-zero amount of duration, but it is much lower than expected by a naive reading on the figures because of this. The Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND), a benchmark bond index ETF, has a duration of 5.8 years, less than 10x that of JPST, but BND suffered around 30x more losses in 2022. Important implication of this is that JPST's rate risk is much, much closer to that of t-bills than bonds, even more than implied in these figures.

Looking at credit quality, JPST focuses on high-quality investments, including government securities and AAA-rated investments. It does invest quite significantly in both A and BBB securities, which are still investment-grade securities, but on the lower-end of the scale.

JPST's overall credit quality is quite high, albeit materially lower than that of t-bills. Expect a non-zero amount of losses during downturns and recessions, higher than t-bills, but lower than riskier investments like high-yield corporate bonds or equities. As an example, JPST's performance during the coronavirus pandemic:

JPST's high-quality, short-term portfolio should experience little volatility moving forward, albeit more than t-bills. Realized volatility is around twice that of t-bills, significantly lower than that of most bonds.

As should be clear from the above, JPST is an incredibly safe, low-volatility investment, albeit riskier, more volatile than t-bills. Seems more than adequate for most risk-averse investors, but those wishing to completely minimize their risk might prefer t-bills and other alternatives to cash over JPST.

Dividend Yield and Performance Track-Record

JPST currently yields 4.5%, slightly higher than the 4.2% t-bill average. It is also a slightly higher yield than most bond ETFs, but mostly because most of these still hold older, lower-yielding bonds from before 2022. Accounting for these older securities, JPST's dividend yield is competitive, but not higher-than-average, if that makes sense.

As JPST focuses on securities with slightly more credit risk and of longer duration than t-bills, it should almost always trade at a spread to these securities. Said spread should ensure long-term outperformance, as has been the case since JPST's inception.

On the flipside, as the fund focuses on securities with less risk than the average bond, it should underperform most bonds and bond sub-asset classes long-term. JPST has outperformed most bonds since inception, better-than-expected results.

Outperformance here is almost entirely due to favorable macro conditions: rates skyrocketed during 2022 and 2023, leading to significant losses for most bonds, negligible losses for very short-term funds like JPST. Short-term bond indexes performed better than above, although JPST still (marginally) outperformed these.

JPST's overall performance track-record is quite strong, even considering the favorable macro conditions of these past few years. Significant rate hikes obviously benefitted the fund, at least relative to peers, but they also benefitted short-term bond index ETFs, and JPST outperformed relative to these too. Still, I would expect JPST to underperform most bonds moving forward, outperform t-bills.

JPST - Quick Peer Comparison

As I've covered several ETFs with broadly similar characteristics to JPST, thought to do a quick comparison between these. Let's start with JAAA.

JPST invests in high-quality, ultra-short term bonds and fixed-income securities. JAAA focuses on AAA-rated CLOs.

JAAA's CLOs are the higher-quality, higher-rating securities, but JPST's bonds are more liquid investments, resulting in lower realized volatility.

Long-term, credit quality matters more, but short-term volatility also matters somewhat and might be of particular importance to more risk-averse investors, or those looking for a place to park their cash.

CLOs consistently trade at a spread to fixed-income securities of comparable credit risk, with JAAA consistently yielding a bit more than JPST.

Due to the above, JAAA should outperform JPST long-term, as has been the case since inception.

Overall, I prefer JAAA's higher-quality, higher-yield holdings, but some investors might prefer JPST's lower realized volatility

BOXX is another solid choice in this space

JPST invests in high-quality, ultra-short term bonds and fixed-income securities, with BOXX using options to achieve returns effectively identical to those of t-bills.

Due to the above, JPST compares to BOXX in much the same way as it does to t-bill: it has a higher yield, higher returns, higher volatility

BOXX does have one significant advantage to both t-bills and JPST: it is much more tax-friendly. Specifically, BOXX's options rarely generate income or realized gains, which should allow investors to defer any and all taxes until they sell their position. No income should mean no distributions, with BOXX paying a single distribution since its inception in late 2022.

BOXX's strategy should result in important tax benefits to some investors, especially those facing higher marginal tax rates in taxable accounts. For other investors, especially those in tax-advantaged retirement accounts, the fund should prove identical to a simple t-bills ETF, minus the lack of distributions.

In my opinion, BOXX is a much more interesting ETF, and much stronger for a specific type of investor. As BOXX's advantages are ultimately dependent on the specific circumstances of each investor, I can't really say it's overall 'better' than JPST, much less recommend one over the other.

Looking Back

As mentioned previously, I've covered JPST several times in the past, and I've generally been bullish. Last article was an exception, as the fund traded with a comparable dividend yield to t-bills at the time, which made outperformance difficult.

JPST's dividend yield recovered by around mid-2024 and was trading with a reasonable spread to t-bills by late 2024.

JPST's performance relative to t-bills has roughly tracked these spreads. Returns were effectively identical in the first half of 2024, with JPST slightly outperforming during the second half.

Wider spreads have led to more material outperformance YTD.

Overall, I think my assessment of JPST was right before. It didn't trade at a spread to t-bills, so investing in the fund meant higher risk without a commensurate increase income or (expected) returns. Once spreads widened, performance improved, as expected.

Conclusion

JPST is an actively-managed bond ETF focusing on high-quality, short-term securities. It is broadly comparable to t-bills, with slightly a slightly higher yield, performance track-record, risk and volatility. It is a simple, effective investment, and should be of particular importance to investors looking for a place to park their cash.

data.title: Nvidia: Now The 2nd Cheapest Stock In The Mag 7
data.description:
NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) just delivered one of the most extraordinary quarters in market history, yet the stock is being treated like a bubble instead of a cash machine. While bears insist the A.I. boom is overheating, the hard data from Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), Alphabet (GOOGL), and Microsoft (MSFT) says the opposite. We have seen backlog for cloud services grow, allocations toward CapEx increase, and the cash from operations that these companies are generating expanding. On Wednesday Afternoon NVDA announced the best earnings results I have ever seen from a company. NVDA is the largest company in the S&P 500 and they just matched Palantir’s (PLTR) YoY growth in Q3. NVDA guided for $54 billion of revenue in the 3rd quarter and delivered $57 billion which was a 62.49% YoY increase and an increase of 22% QoQ.

There are parts of the market that are expensive as some multiples have gotten ahead of themselves but that happens in every market. I don’t believe that NVDA deserved to sell off after the historic quarter and they are now trading at less than 20 times 2027 earnings which is cheaper than The Coca-Cola Company (KO) with roughly 7x the EPS growth over the next 2-years. If NVDA continues to fall I will be adding as I believe this is a great setup for long-term investors. It will be interesting to see what happens when they release Q4 earnings considering they forecasted $65 billion in revenue which would be a YoY increase of 65.26%.

Following up on my previous article about NVIDIA Corporation

At the beginning of September I wrote an article about NVDA (can be read here) where I discussed why it was poised for multi-year growth. Since then shares of NVDA are up 1.77% while the S&P 500 is down -0.75%. I discussed how NVDA’s Q2 results showcased exceptional operational performance, with data center revenue up 56% YoY and profit margins exceeding 50%. I believed that the sustained CapEx allocation from the hyperscalers would continue to benefit NVDA and it’s shares would exceed $200 by the end of the year. Shares of NVDA reached by prediction but the market has been extremely volatile throughout the government shutdown. I believe that NVDA’s results decimated the bear thesis and the dip will get bought as NVDA is now the 2nd cheapest stock in the Magnificent Seven.

Why the bears are wrong and NVDA didn’t deserve to get sold off after earnings

I like to follow the money when I make investment decisions and I don’t believe there is a bubble within the A.I. narrative. The combination of MSFT, META, GOOGL, and AMZN have allocated $97.27 billion toward CapEx in Q3. These companies haven’t taken their foot off the gas as they continue to be in a prisoners dilemma considering they all agree that they can’t add compute quick enough. In Q3 2024 these companies spent $58.86 billion on CapEx and over the past year this has increased by 62.25% ($38.41 billion). I would rather trust Mark Zuckerberg (META CEO), Sundar Pichai (GOOGL CEO), Andy Jassy (AMZN CEO), and Satya Nadella (MSFT CEO) that allocating this amount of capital toward CapEx is the best move to create future value for shareholders. These are the CEOs that have built some of the only companies to ever exceed a $1 trillion market cap. This isn’t a situation where one CEO is going off the reservation as they are all in lockstep with the need for more data centers and compute. This is a situation where you either trust the CEOs that are running these businesses rather than bears sitting on the sidelines trying to call the next financial crisis.

While the bears are screaming over the amount of capital being allocated toward CapEx I believe they are missing the bigger picture as MSFT, META, GOOGL, and AMZN are funding this from profitability. Over the trailing twelve months (TTM) these companies have generated $1.54 trillion in revenue and $549.7 billion in EBITDA. The $329.76 billion in CapEx that they have deployed has come from the cash from operations that their businesses produce unlike in the dot-com bubble when expansion was fueled by venture debt. These companies have generated $536.73 billion in cash from operations and after the CapEx allocation they have still produced $206.97 billion in Free Cash Flow (FCF). When I see this I see sustained spending until the hyperscalers feel that they can go into maintenance mode and we know from the earnings calls that they will be spending more on CapEx next year than they did this year.

I don’t believe that the bears actually do the deep research on the numbers because if they did they would see that Apple (AAPL) who hasn’t invested heavily in CapEx is severely trailing MSFT, META, GOOGL, and AMZN in revenue and cash from operations growth. Since Over the past 5-years AAPL has allocated $54.92 billion toward CapEX while META has spent $176.91 billion, MSFT has spent $186.11 billion, GOOGL has spent $218.78 billion, and AMZN has spent $381.15 billion. This has been a big critique of AAPL because they have feel behind in the A.I. and cloud computing segments and the results show up in their revenue and cash from operations growth.

Since the 2021 fiscal year AAPL has grown its revenue by 13.76% ($50.34 billion) and its cash from operations by 7.16% ($7.44 billion). In 2021 AAPL was the only company that exceeded $104.04 billion in cash from operations and in the TTM they have drastically fallen behind as MSFT, GOOGL, and AMZN all generate more cash from operations than AAPL. MSFT has been able to grow its revenue by 67.61% and its cash from operations by 65.15% since 2021 and is generating $147.04 billion in cash from operations at a 52.19% margin. GOOGL has grown its revenue by 49.62% and its cash from operations by 65.22% to $151.43 billion over this period. The growth in revenue and cash from operations is a direct correlation to investing in themselves through the expansion in CapEx and AAPL is a clear indication of what happens to companies that underinvest and don’t take part in the current A.I. and cloud cycle.

NVDA finished the day down -3.15% (-$5.44) to $180.64 after the best earnings report I have ever seen. Over the past day the combination of 35 analysts have increased NVDA’s 2027 earnings from $8.09 to $9.43 which has made the forward multiple get even more attractive. NVDA is now trading at 19.16 times 2027 earnings and is expected to grow it’s EPS by 106.35% over the next 2-years. There are only 2 companies in the Mag 7 trading under 20 times forward earnings and they are META and NVDA. NVDA is trading at a lower valuation than the Coca Cola Company and they are the largest beneficiaries of the hyperscalers increasing their allocations toward CapEx. Walmart (WMT) and Costco (COST) are trading at more than 30 times 2027 earnings with less than 26% forward EPS over the next 2-years and have larger multiples than all of the Mag 7 except for Tesla (TSLA). I don’t believe that NVDA deserved to selloff after their Q3 earnings as their profitability and revenue is only expected to increase and there is enough supporting evidence that their largest customers are funding all of their purchases from profitability and a lot more capital can get allocated toward CapEx as they continue to become more profitable.

NVIDIA Corporations Q3 earnings were a masterpiece and I am excited for their future

NVDA just posted a blowout quarter where they had originally guided for $54 billion plus or minus 2% and generated $57 billion in revenue which was an increase of 62.49% YoY. There non-GAAP EPS came in at $1.30 which was an increase of 67% YoY and $0.04 ahead of expectations. NVDA’s profitability is the big story considering they posted a gross margin of 73.4% and generating $36 billion in operating income which was an increase of 65% YoY. NVDA is operating at a 63.17% operating margin which is unheard of for a hardware company. On the bottom line NVDA generated $31.91 billion in net income for a 55.98% profit margin. If I put a 52% profit margin on Q4 earnings to be conservative they would produce $34.45 billion in net income next quarter and have a run rate that exceeds $128 billion of net income for 2026. On the earnings call management characterized demand as running ahead of supply and said cloud GPUs are sold out with GPUs across Hopper, Ampere and the new Blackwell generation running at full utilization. This doesn’t seem like a fad to me and the fact that the bears are focusing on the wrong narrative.

NVDA’s Q3 Data Center revenue hit $51.2B which was an increase of 66% YoY and 25% QoQ. An aspect within the headline number is networking revenue which surged to $8.2B from $3.1B a year ago. This was driven by NVLink, InfiniBand and Spectrum-X Ethernet which is critical for A.I. factories. NVDA’s compute revenue grew 56% YoY which was led by the GB 300 Blackwell Ultra ramp as the GB 300 represents roughly two-thirds of Blackwell revenue. NVDA is no longer just selling chips as its architecting entire rack-scale and data-center-scale A.I. factories with Grace-Blackwell systems, Spectrum-X Ethernet, InfiniBand, BlueField DPUs, Omniverse-based digital twins, and CUDA/X software. NVDA is taking minority stakes in key A.I. model companies and are tying their future architectures directly to CUDA and optimizing its road map for future workloads.

NVDA just guided for $65 billion of revenue in Q4 and I think they will continue to expand the quarterly revenue that is generated for the foreseeable future. Each new A.I. generation demands more compute, more memory bandwidth, and more sophisticated networking and NVDA is able to monetize each segment. NVDA is working on building out it’s software and services such as AI Enterprise, Omniverse, digital twins, and robotics platforms to create incremental higher-margin revenue streams that sit on top of the hardware sales. I believe that were at a point where A.I. infrastructure spending will continue to grow globally which will allow NVDA to increase the number of systems deployed and generate more revenue per system through networking, software, and full platforms. This will diversify their revenue mix and create a situation where the hardware is the first layer of revenue then NVDA will reap the benefits of having companies dependent on the software. The Q3 print from NVDA cemented its status as the core infrastructure vendor of the A.I. era.

Risks to investing in NVIDIA Corporation

While NVDA just blew out earnings estimates there are still significant risk factors to consider and investors should do their own due diligence. If the hyperscalers show down their CapEx allocation it will negatively impact NVDA’s forward numbers and could cause a collapse in its share price. There is also the risk that other companies are able to create proprietary chips that reduce their dependance on NVDA which would also impact the forward narrative. NVDA is in a position where they dominate the market so their customers will try to find a way to augment NVDA chips so their not beheld to NVDA indefinitely and if there is a breakthrough in TPUs this could happen much sooner then expected. Please do your own research because NVDA could continue lower even after this historic quarter.

Conclusion

In my opinion, the market is completely mispricing what NVDA has become as this isn’t a cyclical semiconductor story or a short-lived A.I. trade. NVDA has become the core infrastructure provider for a multi-trillion-dollar buildout that’s being funded by the most profitable companies on earth. The hyperscalers are effectively telling us with their CapEx budgets that they expect A.I. demand to keep ramping, and NVDA is sitting in the tollbooth of that expansion with leadership in GPUs, networking, and now full A.I. factories. I believe the Q3 print should go down as the moment the bear narrative broke. NVDA just delivered one of the best quarters in market history, guided to another massive step-up in Q4, and still has demand running ahead of supply across Hopper, Blackwell, and its networking stack. NVDA is now trading at less than 20 times forward earnings and is the 2nd cheapest stock in the Magnificent 7 which I think is a huge discount to their fair value.

data.title: Madison Small Cap Fund Q3 2025 Portfolio Activity
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Madison Small Cap Fund Q3 2025 Investment Strategy Letter.

The third quarter was very difficult for the Small Cap Fund. The Russell 2000 reached a bottom on April 8th and shot upward through the third quarter, returning 12% for the quarter. The Russell 2500 returned 9.0% in the third quarter. The performance of the small-cap index was broad-based, with every sector except Utilities up double-digits. The Madison Small Cap Fund (Class Y)(BVAOX) was down 1.3% in the period, significantly underperforming the benchmarks. This underperformance was primarily the result of stock selection and exacerbated by a very speculative market.

We made several changes to the portfolio in the third quarter.

Buys

Hexcel Corp. (HXL)

The small cap team initiated a new investment position in Hexcel Corporation. Hexcel is a global leader in advanced composite materials, specializing in the production of carbon fiber reinforcements, resin systems, and honeycomb structures. These materials are critical for lightweight, high-performance applications, particularly in the commercial aerospace, space, and defense sectors. Hexcel’s products are used in aircraft frames, wings, engines, and other structural components, offering strength and durability while significantly reducing weight. Major clients include aerospace giants like Airbus and Boeing. We like the duopoly market structure and deep moats of Hexcel’s business. The cycle has been in a prolonged downtrend due to the COVID supply chain shocks, creating bottlenecks in engines and the struggles of Boeing and Spirit Aerosystems. We believe these headwinds are now largely behind us. The competitive moats in this business are significant. Capital intensity is relatively high, while incumbency and vertical integration provide a huge competitive advantage. Certification in wing and fuselage requires massive regulatory approval, which leads to even higher switching costs. Furthermore, the industry has a benign competitive structure, with Japanese firm Toray being the only other direct, scaled composite material competitor, having a combined share in wing and fuselage that approaches 90% and holding sole-sourced positions with original equipment manufacturers (OEMs). We believe the intrinsic value for this franchise is $85.

Eagle Materials (EXP)

Small cap initiated on Eagle Materials, a leading U.S. manufacturer of heavy construction materials such as Portland cement, concrete, and aggregates, and light building materials such as wallboard and recycled paperboard. We view Eagle as an excellent company with very attractive financial traits. EXP aligns well with our preference for high-quality holdings, given its strong margins, high return on invested capital (ROIC), and return on equity (ROE). With the residential construction cycle in a prolonged downturn, we see this as a timely opportunity to invest in Eagle. Demand for wallboard has been declining for several years, and operating rates for the industry are in the 70s, which means they have a lot of excess capacity. Investors, while somewhat mollified by the resiliency of the business, have lost patience with many construction-exposed names. During the downturn, Eagle has gained market share due to its geographical footprint and cost-competitive wallboard assets. Further, we believe we are close to a bottom in residential construction demand, and at this price, we are willing to be patient for the cycle to turn. Management is excellent and allocates capital very judiciously with copious share buybacks. Our current intrinsic value estimate does not assume meaningful margin expansion and has relatively conservative buyback assumptions. We calculate intrinsic value at $269/share.

Charles River Laboratories (CRL)

We initiated a position in Charles River Labs in Q3. CRL is a high-quality company in the drug discovery and development outsourcing space with a broad range of services from basic research to contract manufacturing. The company has operating margins of 20% or more and generates substantial free cash flow. The past few years have been challenging for companies in the drug development space, as capital has become increasingly constrained and research and development spending has remained stagnant. However, CRL has the operating history and mix of businesses to be successful over the long term. The customer base is diverse and CRL benefits from the increased dynamics of outsourcing by its biopharmaceutical clients. The company’s top line has grown at a 12% compound annual rate over the last 10 years, even with the recent downturn. Management is solid, and we’ve known them for decades. We believe Charles River’s earnings power can reach approximately $15 in 2028, assuming modest growth. We also use a sum of the parts at ~12x EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization) to assess the attractiveness of CRL. This implies an intrinsic value of $228. Importantly, we think we are being conservative, as we don’t build in a resumption of double-digit revenue growth in our valuation framework. However, we anticipate revenue growth of 4% from 2024 to 2028 and a modest margin recovery, aided by restructuring and cost-outs.

Sales

GMS, Inc. (GMS)

GMS was acquired by Home Depot after receiving an initial bid from QXO. We received a roughly 45% premium to the unaffected share price.

Saia, Inc. (SAIA)

Small cap exited Saia this quarter. The company has faced a challenging macroeconomic environment for LTL (less-than-truckload) pricing and experienced some operational hiccups as it expanded into new geographies. We would certainly revisit the investment as operating rates can inflect higher in a better environment, which would drive strong earnings leverage. We believe the moat around this business is excellent; we think the company is well-run, with a maniacal focus on customer service.

Madison lists the performance of the Predecessor Fund and accounting survivor of the Reorganization for the following reasons: Continuity of Fund portfolio managers through the Reorganization; Substantially the same investment objective and investment strategies between the Fund and the Predecessor Fund; Substantially similar investment policies between the Fund and the Predecessor Fund; A similar expense ratio (excluding acquired fund fees and expenses).

Madison Asset Management, LLC does not provide investment advice directly to shareholders of the Madison Funds.

Madison Funds are distributed by MFD Distributor, LLC, member of FINRA. Portfolio data is as of the date of this piece unless otherwise noted and holdings are subject to change.

“Madison” and/or “Madison Investments” is the unifying tradename of Madison Investment Holdings, Inc., Madison Asset Management, LLC (“MAM”), and Madison Investment Advisors, LLC (“MIA”). MAM and MIA are registered as investment advisers with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission. Madison Funds are distributed by MFD Distributor, LLC. MFD Distributor, LLC is registered with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission as a broker-dealer and is a member firm of the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority. The home office for each firm listed above is 550 Science Drive, Madison, WI 53711. Madison’s toll-free number is 800-767-0300.

Any performance data shown represents past performance. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

This report is for informational purposes only and is not intended as an offer or solicitation with respect to the purchase or sale of any security and is not investment advice.

Non-deposit investment products are not federally insured, involve investment risk, may lose value and are not obligations of, or guaranteed by, any financial institution. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate.

An investment in the Fund is subject to risk and there can be no assurance the Fund will achieve its investment objective. The risks associated with an investment in the Fund can increase during times of significant market

volatility. The principal risks of investing in the Fund include mid cap risk, equity risk, growth and value risks, capital gains realization risks to taxpaying shareholders, foreign security and emerging market risk, depository receipt risk, and market risk. Investing in small, mid-size or emerging companies involves greater risks not associated with investing in more established companies, such as business risk, significant stock price fluctuations and illiquidity. More detailed information regarding these risks can be found in the Fund’s prospectus.

Upon request, Madison may furnish to the client or institution a list of all security recommendations made within the past year.

Indices are unmanaged. An investor cannot invest directly in an index. They are shown for illustrative purposes only, and do not represent the performance of any specific investment. Index returns do not include any expenses, fees or sales charges, which would lower performance.

Russell 2000 Index is a small cap market index which measures the performance of the smallest 2,000 companies in the Russell 3000® Index.

Russell 2500 Index is a broad index, featuring 2,500 stocks that cover the small and mid-cap market capitalizations of the U.S. equity universe.

Russell Investment Group is the source and owner of the trademarks, service marks and copyrights related to the Russell Indexes. Russell® is a trademark of Russell Investment Group.

EPS Growth Rate (3-5 year): is the annual rate at which a company’s earnings are expected to grow.

Price-to-Earnings Ratio: measures how expensive a stock is. It is calculated by the weighted average of a stock’s current price divided by the company’s earnings per share of stock in a portfolio.

Active Share: the percentage of a portfolio that differs from its benchmark index. It can range from 0% for an index fund that perfectly mirrors its benchmark to 100% for a portfolio with no overlap with an index.

Portfolio Turnover: a measure of the trading activity in an investment portfolio—how often securities are bought and sold by a portfolio. The range represents the typical turnover of the portfolio.

Wtd Avg. Market Cap: the size of the companies in which the fund invests. Market capitalization is calculated by number of a company’s shares outstanding times its price per share.

Standard Deviation: a statistical measurement of dispersion about an average, which, for a portfolio, depicts how widely the returns varied over a certain period of time. Investors may use the standard deviation of historical performance to understand the range of returns for a portfolio. When a portfolio has a higher standard deviation than its benchmark, it implies higher relative volatility. Standard deviation has been calculated using the trailing monthly total returns for the appropriate time period. The standard deviation values are annualized.

Upside Capture Ratio: a fund’s performance in up markets relative to its benchmark. The security’s upside capture return is divided by the benchmark’s upside capture return over the time period.

Downside Capture Ratio: a fund’s performance in down markets relative to its benchmark. The security’s downside capture return is divided by the benchmark’s downside capture return over the time period.

Beta: a measure of the fund’s sensitivity to market movements. A portfolio with a beta greater than 1 is more volatile than the market, and a portfolio with a beta less than 1 is less volatile than the market.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Enjoy Monthly Income From These Great Picks; Yields +5.5%
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

When we were kids, becoming an adult was often the goal because it was seen as a pathway to freedom to make our own choices. After all, adults get to decide their bedtime, their meal preferences, set their own schedules, and spend their money however they please; I could eat candy all day long, and no one can stop me! But as we grow up, we realize that freedom comes at a price. That independence we sought after comes with a steady stream of responsibilities – rent or mortgage payments, utility and phone bills, insurance premiums, subscription renewals, those bills just keep piling up.

The truth is, those bills aren’t going anywhere. They arrive like clockwork, so your income needs to do the same. You have two choices – you can keep working, to keep the income flowing in order to tackle those bills, or you can make your money generate passive income, reliably and consistently.

“If you don't find a way to make money while you sleep, you will work until you die.” – Warren Buffett

No one can keep working forever. If your income can keep flowing while you take a break, that’s when you truly achieve financial freedom. With that in mind, let’s look at our top monthly-paying securities for a retirement on your terms.

Pick #1: BGR – Yield 8.7%

Energy prices have been volatile over the past decade. Following the oil crash in 2016, which drove several small-scale drillers out of business in key basins in the U.S., the industry was hit by another shock from the global pandemic, which brought several transportation-dependent businesses to a standstill. Larger companies operating in this industry learned a hard lesson after 2016, and focused on improving their balance sheets in the years ahead. This was a key reason they were better positioned to recover from the COVID-19 period of record-low oil prices.

Larger energy companies continue to operate cautiously and maintain financial discipline. They have shifted from the mindset of “production growth at any cost” to more shareholder-friendly practices. As such, we expect the next ten years for Big Energy to be brighter than the past decade.

BlackRock Energy & Resources Trust (BGR) is a CEF that strategically invests at least 80% of its assets in energy and natural resources companies. BGR’s top holdings are some of the largest E&P, Midstream, and downstream corporations in North America. American and Canadian companies represent ~88% of the fund’s invested assets, and BGR does not use leverage as part of its investment strategy. Source

A $10,000 investment in BGR made in Dec 2015 would be worth slightly more than that today, but the fund has delivered over $6,500 in total distributions to date. If we look at the distribution breakdown over the past five years, we see a healthy balance between Qualified Dividend Income and Return of Capital, making BGR an incredibly tax-advantageous income producer for eligible investors.

Before you set your hair on fire regarding the usage of ROC, we will note that the CEF has maintained its NAV over the past decade, aside from the sharp drop due to the aforementioned COVID-19 oil shock, which was a terrific buying opportunity across the hydrocarbon energy industry. This shows that the fund has sustainably earned its distributions over this time.

According to BGR’s semi-annual report, the fund generated $17.2 million in NII, while paying out $14.9 million to shareholders in the form of distributions. The CEF reported $87.4 million in unrealized gains as of June 30, 2025, adequate to support almost 3 years of distributions. It does not come as a surprise that BGR raised its distribution late last year.

Hydrocarbons have a bright future for the decades ahead, and BGR pays $0.0973/share on a monthly basis to support your income needs from this incredibly sticky and time-tested industry. BGR’s payout reflects an 8.7% yield at today's prices, positioning shareholders for strong returns in the years ahead.

Pick #2: O – Yield 5.7%

Realty Income Corporation (O) reported its third-quarter earnings on November 3rd, with earnings coming in largely in line with expectations. The 6th-largest global REIT ended Q3 with 15,542 properties in nine countries, with Triple-Net Lease agreements with 1,647 tenants, operating across 92 industries. 31.5% of its tenants are investment-grade, and this is notably down from 33.9% in Q2 due to the fact that in July, investment-grade Dollar Tree sold Family Dollar to private asset managers – Brigade Capital Management and Macellum Capital Management. Family Dollar is one of O’s largest tenants, comprising 2.7% of the ABR (Annualized Base Rent). This discount retailer is privately held, and no longer has official investment-grade ratings. Source

During Q3, O continued to demonstrate its scale and strength, pursuing $1.4 billion in acquisitions at a 7.7% weighted-average cap rate. This brings the YTD total to $3.9 billion, and the REIT has raised its investment volume guidance for 2025 to $5.5 billion (up from $5 billion prior guidance). Almost $1 billion of these acquisitions were focused on the E.U. markets, and this market now represents 18% of Realty Income’s ABR.

O reported to have sold 140 properties in Q3 to generate $215 million in net proceeds, highlighting the notable disposition of 18 convenience store assets at a blended cap rate of 5.5%. This indicates the substantial spread between its disposition and acquisition cap rates. O ended the third quarter with 98.7% occupancy level, and has guided a 98.5% occupancy level for the end of 2025.

During the conference call, management narrowed their 2025 AFFO guidance to $4.25 - $4.27/share, and this places the REIT’s dividend at a 75% payout ratio. Management also modestly raised the target occupancy level (to 98.5%), and the expected acquisition volume (to $5.5 billion). Realty Income continues to exhibit dominance in the Net-Lease REIT industry, while paying growing dividends to shareholders. Its 5.6% yield may appear small, but for investors who value consistency over speculation, Realty Income, with its deeply diversified business, its high-quality portfolio and industry leadership provide much-needed stability and reliability that your portfolio deserves,

Conclusion

Life doesn’t pause, and neither do your bills. So your income can’t take a break either. By investing in high-quality income-focused securities, you’re setting up a system where money flows in without you putting in the regular effort, allowing you to focus on living life on your terms.

Whether you’re planning for retirement, covering your everyday expenses, or simply seeking greater consistency in your cash flow, income securities can provide much-needed peace of mind and stability.

At High Dividend Opportunities, we specialize in identifying dependable, income-generating investments, designed to help you earn while you sleep. Because true freedom isn’t just about growing up, it’s about growing your income, passively.

data.title: Alibaba: Bearish Catalysts Brewing Before Earnings
data.description:
Alibaba (BABA) has performed well in 2025 so far in terms of share price growth, with an 80% rally year-to-date, meaning that my January 2025 bearish call did not age well. I underappreciated BABA's AI potential, which was the main growth driver this year. The company reports its FQ2 2026 earnings next week, and I think that there are fundamental reasons to still avoid BABA before earnings. One of the biggest reasons to be cautious is its valuation, which does not look good from the multiples point of view. Moreover, Alibaba's earnings surprise record is extremely weak for such a large company. Continued hype around AI looks like the only reason why the stock might continue rising after the earnings release. However, Alibaba's rapid AI revenue growth doesn't convert into consistent EPS growth. Therefore, I think that BABA is still a 'Strong Sell.'

BABA FQ2 2026 earnings preview

Alibaba releases its FQ2 2026 earnings next week, November 25. There are a few reasons to remain quite cautious before the upcoming earnings release. Earnings revisions usually tell us quite a lot before earnings releases because it reflects the collective opinion of Wall Street analysts. From this perspective, Alibaba is highly likely to either underperform in FQ2 2026 or share cautious guidance for the remainder of the current fiscal year. The picture is not even mixed but has a clear bearish pattern, with 92% of the revisions being negative.

Alibaba's weak earnings surprise record is another solid reason to be cautious about the upcoming earnings release. Four EPS misses over the last eight quarters might be fine for a young, rapidly growing company that still has problems in managing growth. However, such a weak earnings record for an established company like BABA looks like something structural rather than just coincidence. Moreover, it were not just one-cent misses but quite wide ones. Finally, two previous quarters were marked with not only EPS misses but double misses. It is another bearish setup, in my opinion.

If we zoom out, Alibaba's quarterly EPS is another area of concern. The company's bottom line is extremely volatile, and its EPS trendline looks more like a roller-coaster rather than one demonstrated by a company with operating leverage. For example, Amazon's (AMZN) EPS has been growing steadily every quarter since the early 2022 dip and is currently higher compared to the pandemic period. Meanwhile, BABA's EPS is currently below 2021 levels, and this extreme bottom-line volatility is expected to remain over the next several quarters. I could have understood this volatility if Alibaba was ramping up its R&D-to-revenue ratio, but it actually narrowed from almost 8% in FY2021 to sub-6% TTM.

Therefore, I do not see much value for shareholders from Alibaba's soaring AI revenues. Alibaba's AI potential has been one of the main factors of this year's rally. However, we see that rapid growth of AI revenues does not convert into improved bottom-line profitability. Moreover, Alibaba's outstanding share count has been quite stable over the last several quarters, which means that the EPS volatility is not due to changes in the denominator. Therefore, the EPS instability is due to performance across key profitability metrics like gross and operating margins. If we check BABA's income statement over the last several quarters, we can see that both these metrics were extremely unstable.

Last but not least, there is macroeconomic context. As the country's largest e-commerce player, Alibaba's financial performance heavily depends on the broader economy's health. According to CNBC, China's GDP grew by 5.4% in Q1 2025, by 5.2% in Q2 2025, and by 4.8% in Q3 2025. Thus, the country's economic growth is slowing down if we look at this important headline metric. In addition, the below chart shows us that three crucial macroeconomic indicators demonstrated weakness over the last few months. Therefore, Alibaba's core e-commerce business operates in a challenging macroeconomic environment, and it is a solid headwind.

With all these warning signs, I think that it is reasonable to be cautious ahead of Alibaba's upcoming earnings release, both in terms of the FQ2 2026 performance and the management's guidance for the remainder of the current fiscal year.

Valuation analysis

Alibaba's 5-year average P/E ratio is around 14, which is slightly below the sector median. At the moment, the forward P/E ratio of 22 is far above the historical average. Even with all the optimism around AI-related revenue growth, I think that a 60% gap between current P/E and historical average is not justified. The reason why I think so is because Alibaba's rapidly growing AI-related revenue does not convert into consistent EPS growth despite the R&D-to-revenue ratio not demonstrating growth.

Alibaba is also overvalued in terms of the Price/FCF ratio. The gap is approximately the same as in the case with the P/E ratio. With the EPS not expected to demonstrate aggressive growth over the next few quarters, it is also difficult to expect the Price/FCF ratio contraction.

Looking at the forward P/E ratio setup might be misleading because a notable contraction between FY2026 and FY2028 is expected. However, in absolute numbers, the estimated FY2028 forward P/E is approximately on the same level as BABA's historical non-GAAP P/E ratio.

We could have said that BABA is undervalued if its estimated FY2028 forward P/E ratio was significantly below the historical average. For example, if it was in high single digits. However, the actual setup is different.

Mitigating factors

I think that AI hype is the biggest risk to my bearish thesis. Alibaba demonstrated strong AI-related revenue growth over the last few quarters, which made investors very bullish. Moreover, the company releases new AI-related features quite frequently, which supports investors' interests in BABA as an AI play.

One of such feature is the new chatbot Qwen, which has the potential not only to compete with direct rivals like ChatGPT or Google's Gemini but can also add synergies to the e-commerce business. Agentic AI features are likely to be added to support on Taobao, the shopping platform. It is difficult to say whether Qwen will be able to really compete with first-movers like ChatGPT or Gemini. However, the synergetic effect due to the incorporation of chatbot's AI capabilities into e-commerce business might happen because 'AI in e-commerce' is a rapidly growing industry, meaning that it really creates value for marketplaces.

Moreover, during the upcoming earnings call, the management might announce new big AI-related projects. The probability is quite high because Alibaba is well-equipped financially to invest both in R&D and M&A. A $23 billion net cash position is a formidable asset, and Alibaba has been quite aggressive in their AI-related investments this year. New project announcements during the earnings call might be met with optimism by investors.

And positive sentiment is also likely to be supported by the fact that Wall Street analysts believe that BABA is significantly undervalued. Despite quite high multiples, the average target price is just slightly below $200, which means that analysts see solid upside potential from current share price levels.

Conclusion

I think there are much more reasons to be bearish about Alibaba ahead of FQ2 2026 earnings than positive factors. Reiterating 'Strong Sell' looks fair considering BABA's high multiples and poor earnings surprise record.
